intro:
Adoption (Greek huiothesia, literally “placing as a son”) is the gracious act by which God receives those who were strangers and enemies into his own family, giving them the name, standing, and full privileges of his children. It is a familial and legal metaphor for salvation: where justification declares the believer righteous and regeneration imparts new life, adoption confers relationship — a place in the household of God with all the intimacy, security, and inheritance that sonship entails. It is the high privilege of the gospel that “we should be called children of God; and so we are” (1 John 3:1).

The roots run deep in the Old Testament. Scripture records human adoptions — Pharaoh’s daughter taking the infant Moses (Exodus 2:10) and Mordecai raising his cousin Esther (Esther 2:7) — but its theological weight rests on God’s claim upon Israel: “Israel is my firstborn son” (Exodus 4:22), “out of Egypt I called my son” (Hosea 11:1), “you are the sons of the LORD your God” (Deuteronomy 14:1). The Davidic king embodied this sonship in a focused way — “I will be to him a father, and he shall be to me a son” (2 Samuel 7:14; Psalms 2:7) — a promise the New Testament sees fulfilled in Christ. Paul accordingly lists “the adoption” first among Israel’s covenant privileges (Romans 9:4).

The doctrine flowers in Paul, who draws on the Greco-Roman practice in which an adopted son received a new name, was freed from former debts, and became a legal heir. God “predestined us for adoption to himself as sons through Jesus Christ” (Ephesians 1:5); in the fullness of time he sent his Son “to redeem those who were under the law, so that we might receive adoption as sons,” and then sent “the Spirit of his Son into our hearts, crying, ‘Abba! Father!’” (Galatians 4:4-6). That same Spirit is “the Spirit of adoption,” who frees believers from the slavery of fear and bears witness that they are God’s children — “and if children, then heirs … fellow heirs with Christ” (Romans 8:15-17). John expresses the same reality through new birth: “to all who did receive him … he gave the right to become children of God” (John 1:12).

Adoption is therefore grounded wholly in grace through union with the eternal Son: he is Son by nature, believers sons by gift, sharing the standing that is his by right. Its blessings are present — the cry of Abba, the assurance of belonging, fatherly discipline and care (Hebrews 12:5-11) — yet its consummation is future, for creation itself waits as the children of God await “adoption as sons, the redemption of our bodies” at the resurrection (Romans 8:23). What it will fully mean has not yet appeared, “but we know that when he appears we shall be like him” (1 John 3:2).
